title: The Go-To DevOps Language
description:
## Picking a Language for Automation

You've inherited a pile of one-off automation: provisioning scripts, log parsers, glue between APIs, deployment helpers, and small CLI tools. You want a **single language** that the broader DevOps community reaches for first — readable, ubiquitous on Linux, and backed by a huge ecosystem of libraries for automation and cloud APIs.

Which language is the best single answer?

options.text: Python — readable, pre-installed on most Linux distros, and with a vast ecosystem (requests, boto3, Ansible, etc.) for automation and cloud tooling.
Bash only — every task should be a shell script, no matter how complex.
Java — its verbosity and JVM startup make it the natural fit for quick glue scripts.
C++ — manual memory management makes it ideal for everyday automation chores.

explanation: **Python** is the most common go-to language for DevOps automation and glue code. It's highly readable, ships on virtually every Linux distribution, and has an enormous ecosystem for exactly this work (HTTP via `requests`, cloud SDKs like `boto3`, configuration management like Ansible which is itself written in Python). It hits the sweet spot between throwaway shell scripts and full applications. **Bash** (b) is essential for short pipelines and gluing commands together, but it becomes fragile and hard to maintain for anything with real logic, data structures or error handling. **Java** (c) is verbose with slow startup — fine for large services, awkward for quick scripts. **C++** (d) is a systems language; its manual memory management and compile cycle make it a poor fit for routine automation. (Note: **Go** is also extremely popular for building cloud-native *tooling* and binaries — Docker, Kubernetes and Terraform are written in Go — but as the single best answer for everyday scripting/glue, Python wins.)

hints.title: Scripting vs. building
hints.body: Distinguish 'quick automation and glue' (favors a readable, batteries-included scripting language) from 'building a distributed system binary' (favors Go). The question asks about the former.
